data_source: Nucor FY1999 10-K complete submission, SEC EDGAR; Berkshire 13F-HR/A for quarter ended 2000-03-31

g4_revenue_trend.description: Sales slipped from $4.18B in 1997 to $4.15B in 1998 to $4.01B in 1999, but this was cyclical steel pricing pressure rather than evidence that Nucor's business model was structurally deteriorating.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: Gross margin compressed from about 14.5% in 1997 to 13.5% in 1998 to 13.2% in 1999 as steel conditions softened. That is a real two-year decline, even if it was cyclical rather than existential.

g7_moat.description: Nucor had a real moat by steel standards: a low-cost mini-mill footprint, decentralized operating culture, flexible labor model, and management discipline that let it outperform integrated competitors across cycles. The moat was real, but it was still a moat inside a cyclical commodity business, not a tollbooth immune to price swings.

quality_multiple_note.summary: Nucor was not actually expensive in an absolute sense, but the earnings-yield hurdle still failed once enterprise value was adjusted for the market price implied by Berkshire's disclosed Q1 2000 holding.
quality_multiple_note.reason_for_low_yield: The sub-6% yield did not reflect hype. It reflected a fair price for the best operator in a cyclical steel industry whose margins were already compressing.
quality_multiple_note.decision_impact: This note reinforces the PASS rather than softening it. Nucor was an admirable steel company, but not the kind of high-certainty compounding franchise the framework wants to underwrite as a Buffett-grade long hold.

management_veto.summary: Management quality and culture were strengths, not concerns. The issue was industry economics and valuation discipline, not trust.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — an integrated steelmaker with weaker economics, less flexible cost structure, and no Nucor-style operating moat.
PASS — more leveraged sheet-steel exposure with lower resilience and no clear Nucor-equivalent culture advantage.

buffett_action: By March 31, 2000 Berkshire disclosed 1,267,900 Nucor shares valued at about $63.4M via a confidential-treatment 13F amendment.
actual_result: Nucor remained the best-managed major U.S. steelmaker and later had strong cycles, but it did not become a defining Berkshire-style long-duration compounding franchise.
key_insight: The best company in a cyclical commodity industry can still be a pass if margin compression and only-fair valuation leave too little room for Buffett-style long-hold certainty.
